docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:5:<p class="doc-intro__lead">This is the quant handoff decision in the workflow. Static SVI repair can clean up each expiry and still fail Dupire if the maturity direction leaves seam stress in total-variance derivatives.</p>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:6:<p class="doc-intro__support">The page therefore defends one explicit bridge: preserve the exact calibrated nodes for inspection, then project into a continuous eSSVI surface only because Dupire needs analytic <code>w</code>, <code>w_y</code>, <code>w_yy</code>, and especially <code>w_T</code>. The win is a usable derivative object; the tradeoff is accepting a bounded projection error instead of pretending piecewise time interpolation is good enough.</p>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:10:  <span class="doc-pill">Explicit Dupire bridge</span>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:33:<p class="doc-section-lead">This is the handoff review object: one continuous eSSVI surface for the Dupire bridge, the seam-jump evidence that justifies the projection, and the analytic <code>w_T</code> slices that show the page is targeting derivatives rather than cosmetic implied-vol smoothing.</p>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:49:eSSVI is used here because the Dupire bridge needs analytic <code>w</code>, <code>w_y</code>, <code>w_yy</code>, and <code>w_T</code> together with explicit admissibility checks. A generic slice stack can match nodes without giving a clean derivative object in <code>T</code>.
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:54:Dupire consumes derivatives of total variance. The target is therefore continuity in <code>w(T, y)</code> and especially stable <code>w_T</code>, not cosmetic smoothing of an implied-vol heatmap.
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:64:The bridge accepts <code>price_rmse = 0.02494</code> and <code>max_abs_price_error = 0.11453</code> so Dupire receives a zero-invalid-count smooth surface instead of exact but kinked time interpolation.
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:70:<p class="doc-section-lead">Naive handoff fails when exact nodal agreement is treated as sufficient. Dupire is sensitive to the maturity direction, so a surface can look calm expiry by expiry while still carrying mathematically important kinks in <code>w_T</code>.</p>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:78:This page starts after static repair, not before it. The repaired slices are already the inspectable reference object, the market context is fixed, and the remaining question is whether the maturity direction is smooth enough for a Dupire-oriented handoff.
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:97:| Preserved object | `ESSVINodalSurface(fit.nodes)` | Keep the exact calibrated nodes reviewable before any Dupire-oriented tradeoff is accepted |
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:99:| Quantity smoothed | total variance in maturity | Target the object Dupire differentiates rather than smoothing implied vol for display polish |
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:111:[![](../assets/generated/dupire/localvol_gatheral_heatmap.light.png){ .diagram-img .diagram-light } ![](../assets/generated/dupire/localvol_gatheral_heatmap.dark.png){ .diagram-img .diagram-dark } <span class="proof-path-lightbox-hint" aria-hidden="true">Open larger view</span>](../assets/generated/dupire/localvol_gatheral_heatmap.light.png){ .proof-path-lightbox-trigger data-proof-path-lightbox="" data-light-src="../../assets/generated/dupire/localvol_gatheral_heatmap.light.png" data-dark-src="../../assets/generated/dupire/localvol_gatheral_heatmap.dark.png" data-alt="Heatmap of Gatheral local volatility extracted from the smoothed eSSVI surface" data-lightbox-title="Gatheral local-vol heatmap" aria-label="Open a larger view of the Gatheral local-vol heatmap" aria-describedby="essvi-support-caption-localvol" aria-haspopup="dialog" }
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:124:| Projection tradeoff | `price_rmse = 0.02494`; `max_abs_price_error = 0.11453` | the bridge accepts bounded projection error instead of pretending exact node interpolation is enough for Dupire |
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:125:| Dupire readiness | `projection_dupire_invalid_count = 0` | the projected surface stays admissible for the next local-vol step |
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:129:<p class="doc-section-lead">The smoothed handoff is a deliberate bridge into Dupire, not an attempt to maximize every metric at once.</p>
docs\user_guides\essvi_smooth_handoff.md:133:The workflow keeps two objects on purpose: the exact nodal eSSVI surface for inspection and the smoothed eSSVI surface for Dupire-oriented use. That separation is the mathematical judgment on display. Exact nodes are not discarded, but the next stage receives the continuous surface because analytic <code>w_T</code> matters more here than preserving every seam of the slice stack.
docs\user_guides\essvi.md:27:- `ESSVISmoothedSurface`: smooth projection of nodal parameters for time-differentiable workflows such as Dupire local vol
docs\user_guides\essvi.md:33:- use `ESSVISmoothedSurface` when you need `w_T` and a Dupire-oriented continuous surface
docs\user_guides\essvi.md:165:- parametric admissibility checks such as Lee and Gatheral-Jacquier margins

docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:54:## Heston implementation rationale
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:56:The Heston Fourier pricer needs more than a one-off integral approximation. In
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:73:quadrature rule is universally best across all Heston regimes.
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:89:The Heston diagnostics pages use these signals as review evidence, not as
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:104:Gautschi gives the standard computational route from orthogonal-polynomial
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:109:This repo does not require the Heston docs to rederive that construction in
docs\notes\diagnostics\integration_quadrature.md:118:- *Walter Gautschi - Orthogonal Polynomials - Computation and Approximation (2004).pdf*

docs\notes\volatility\interpolation.md:33:4. Run monotone cubic (PCHIP/Fritsch–Carlson style) in \(T\).
docs\notes\volatility\interpolation.md:43:Even if you interpolate \(w\) nicely in \(T\), **Dupire local vol is sensitive to second derivatives in strike**. Piecewise cubic Hermite methods are \(C^1\) but generally not \(C^2\) at knots, so curvature can jump — which can show up as noisy local vol if you differentiate the interpolant.
docs\notes\volatility\interpolation.md:94:**Why it “levels up”:** You get a curve that is still arbitrage-safe but **much smoother** than piecewise-linear, which matters if you later differentiate (densities, Dupire inputs, etc.).
docs\notes\volatility\interpolation.md:99:monotone cubic interpolation, no-arbitrage spline construction, PAV regression,

docs\notes\pricing\mc.md:147:- *Shreve - Stochastic Calculus for Finance I.pdf*
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:1:# Heston stochastic volatility
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:5:    Heston implementation notes. Repository-specific numerical policy and
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:6:    validation evidence are separated into the adjacent Heston notes.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:8:The Heston model is a standard extension of Black–Scholes when one constant volatility is no longer enough. It keeps continuous-time diffusion pricing, but lets the variance itself evolve randomly and lets spot and variance shocks be correlated. This is one of the simplest stochastic-volatility models that can generate persistent skew and smile while still leaving European vanilla pricing semi-analytic.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:18:- [Dupire local vol](../local-vol-pde/dupire_local_vol.md)
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:25:\(\sigma_{\mathrm{loc}}(S,t)\). Heston instead treats variance itself as a stochastic state variable.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:39:In Heston and Gatheral derivations it is also common to use
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:59:Heston keeps diffusion pricing, replaces constant volatility with a stochastic variance process, correlates spot and variance shocks, and preserves an affine structure that keeps European vanilla pricing tractable.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:81:Heston’s original paper reaches the square-root variance process by first specifying an OU process for \(\sqrt{v_t}\). For pricing and implementation, it is cleaner to write the CIR-style variance process directly.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:103:## 3. Why Heston needs a volatility-risk premium
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:109:That is why Heston’s original PDE contains a volatility-risk term. If \(U(S,v,t)\) denotes the claim value, the PDE contains
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:121:Heston motivates a proportional specification such as
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:141:Under the original Heston setup, a sufficiently smooth claim \(U(S,v,t)\) satisfies a two-factor PDE of the form
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:175:Gatheral often writes
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:181:with \(x = \log(F_{t,T}/K)\). In that notation, Gatheral’s \(P_0\) plays the same role as Heston’s \(P_2\).
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:195:In the Heston model, the affine variance process implies an exponential-affine transform for the relevant log-price characteristic functions:
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:217:Model-intuition note: in the Heston setup, negative \(\rho\) is the parameter
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:220:correlation/skew link as practitioner intuition from the Heston/Gatheral model
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:223:## 9. Notation map: Heston vs Gatheral
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:233:Gatheral’s Chapter 2 variance process is often written as
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:242:\lambda_{\text{Gatheral}} = \kappa,
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:253:1. Heston’s volatility-risk premium \(\lambda_v(S,v,t)\),
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:254:2. Gatheral’s mean-reversion speed \(\lambda\).
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:265:Gatheral often uses
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:289:Equivalent formulas are not always numerically equivalent. The main issue is the complex logarithm inside the Heston characteristic function. A naive principal-branch evaluation can produce discontinuities when the argument crosses the negative real axis.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:298:This is why the repository follows the Gatheral-style algebraic rearrangement
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:300:[Heston Fourier diagnostics](heston_fourier_diagnostics.md). That is an
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:306:A first Heston implementation can focus on European vanillas only:
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:339:This keeps the optimizer unconstrained in raw space while the transform guarantees the fitted Heston parameters remain inside the practical interval for all five parameters. No SciPy optimizer-side bounds are needed in this mode.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:345:The Heston model is an affine stochastic-volatility extension of Black–Scholes in which variance mean reverts, spot and variance shocks are correlated, and European vanilla prices are recovered from characteristic functions through a Black–Scholes-like decomposition.
docs\notes\heston\heston_stochastic_vol.md:347:Heston’s original paper emphasizes the economics and PDE structure. Gatheral emphasizes the risk-neutral implementation route and the stable numerical form of the characteristic function. These are two views of the same model.

docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:1:# Andersen's QE Heston simulation scheme
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:22:That is the real simulation problem for Heston path generation.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:26:The Heston model is
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:45:The whole point is to minimize **discretization bias** in Monte Carlo simulation of the Heston model.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:53:In Heston, the second piece can be large at practical step sizes if the discretization is too naive.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:55:## Big idea behind Andersen's approach
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:141:Because the quadratic branch works for $\psi \le 2$ and the exponential branch works for $\psi \ge 1$, Andersen introduces a threshold
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:204:The exact representation Andersen starts from is
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:273:So Euler here is not principled enough for production-quality Heston simulation.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:293:That is why Andersen abandons naive Euler discretization for $\log X$ and keeps the explicit
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:306:So Andersen introduces a corrected constant $K_0^*$.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:348:Andersen notes that, roughly, positive correlation imposes a step-size restriction of the form
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:454:- Use Broadie--Kaya as an exact-simulation reference in literature discussions,
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:474:- Treating Broadie--Kaya as practical production default misses its complexity and bump-and-reprice inconveniences.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:475:- Treating Euler as anything more than a reference scheme in serious Heston MC is usually a mistake.
docs\notes\heston\heston_qe_notes.md:479:QE works because it respects the shape of the Heston variance transition much better than Euler, and the Andersen $X$ step preserves the correct variance-price coupling by building from the exact representation rather than from naive correlated Gaussian shocks.

docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:1:# Heston Pricing Conventions
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:4:    This note gives model background and notation for the repository's Heston implementation.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:8:This note fixes the pricing conventions used by the Heston Fourier pricer in this repository. It is intended to remove ambiguity around the state variable, log-moneyness input, probability indices, discounting convention, and call/put formulas.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:10:The purpose is not to re-derive the full Heston model. The purpose is to make the implementation contract explicit enough that pricing, calibration, diagnostics, and Monte Carlo validation can all refer to the same convention.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:16:Under the risk-neutral pricing measure, the Heston model is
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:50:This is intentional. The Heston model can be priced outside the Feller region.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:83:The Heston Fourier integrals are implemented in a forward-normalized convention. Prices are finally discounted by \(D(0,T)\).
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:103:The public Heston pricers compute this internally from the market context and strike:
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:131:where \(A_j(u,T)\) is the Heston affine factor for probability index \(j\).
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:139:The repository uses two semantic Heston probabilities:
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:256:This should be a permanent regression test across representative Heston parameter regimes.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:280:In this limit, Heston prices should collapse to Black-76 prices with equivalent volatility
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:315:safeguard for the Cui-style analytic gradients, not a financial lower bound.
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:330:Analytic Heston parameter Jacobians are default-on only for the production
docs\notes\heston\heston_pricing_conventions.md:418:Thus, under this convention, call and put parameter Jacobians have the same Heston-parameter derivative expression for the same strike and maturity.
